name: Prompt Engineering
scope: A person with BASIC proficiency in Prompt Engineering can read a production system prompt critically and identify under-specified instructions, missing edge-case handling, vague output formats, and weak or missing in-context examples. They understand the anatomy of a production prompt — role/persona, task definition, output schema, format constraints, edge-case rules, tone/safety guardrails, and worked examples — and can rewrite a weak prompt to fix specific issues without breaking the working parts. They can read a small traces file (input → model output → judge verdict) and link concrete failures back to specific instructions in the prompt, distinguishing prompt-level issues (under-specified rules, ambiguous schema) from data-level issues (out-of-distribution input, ground truth disagreement). They can compare two prompt variants side-by-side using A/B test results and judge scores, recognising when a higher headline win rate masks regressions on important sub-segments. They can author a prompt iteration plan — a short list of concrete, defensible changes (add a missing example, tighten output format, add an edge-case rule) — with a one-line rationale per change tied to evidence in the traces. They understand basic output format constraints (JSON schema, max length, structured fields), simple guardrails (don't speculate, don't reveal internal labels), and the trade-off between being prescriptive in the prompt vs handling edge cases in calling code. They are aware that prompts must respect external constraints (style guides, output schemas, downstream system contracts) and that a 'better' prompt is one that reduces specific failure modes without breaking those contracts. At BASIC level they do not need to design eval frameworks from scratch, write LLM-judge prompts, propose statistical A/B test methodologies, or fine-tune models — but they do need to read prompts and traces with real attention and produce a concrete next step.
long_scope:
{"sections":[{"name":"Anatomy of a Production Prompt","scope_included":"- Understands the standard sections of a production prompt and what each is for
  - Role / persona framing — who the model is acting as, what product it is part of
  - Task definition — what the model is being asked to do on each call
  - Output schema or format constraints — exact structure, length limits, field definitions
  - In-context examples — at least one worked input → expected output example
  - Edge-case rules — what to do with empty inputs, multi-intent inputs, OOD cases, very long inputs
  - Tone / safety guardrails — language style, things to avoid, refusal rules
- Recognises a stub or under-specified prompt
  - Knows a one-line 'You are a helpful assistant' prompt is not a production prompt
  - Spots when a section is missing entirely vs present but vague
- Knows that prompts evolve — there is no perfect prompt, only the next defensible iteration","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Reading a Prompt Critically","scope_included":"- Reads a production prompt line-by-line and identifies plausible weaknesses without being told where they are
  - Spots instructions that assume one-intent-per-input when real inputs can have multiple
  - Spots label sets or output schemas with no description of how to handle ambiguous or out-of-distribution cases
  - Spots edge cases that are clearly relevant to the domain but not addressed (international/multi-language inputs, very long inputs, attachments, missing source data)
  - Spots tone/format constraints that say nothing about factual accuracy or unsafe content
  - Spots output schemas that work for the happy path but break when source data is missing
- Distinguishes 'prompt is wrong' from 'prompt is fine, calling system is wrong'
  - Knows that not every model failure is a prompt failure — some belong in retrieval, validation, or routing
- Cites the specific line or section of the prompt that is at issue, not just 'the prompt is bad'","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Reading Traces and Linking Failures to Prompt Issues","scope_included":"- Reads a small traces file (input → model output → reference / true label → judge score → judge notes)
  - Computes per-group failure rate when the file has a case_type column
  - Identifies which failures share a common pattern (all multi-intent, all non-English, all missing-field cases)
- Links each pattern back to a specific gap in the prompt
  - 'These three rows all failed because the prompt never tells the model how to handle a customer who is asking two things at once'
  - 'These two rows failed because the schema requires `eta_date` but the input has no date'
- Distinguishes:
  - Prompt-level failures (the prompt does not address this case)
  - Data-level failures (the input is genuinely OOD, no prompt could fix it)
  - Judge-level failures (the judge is scoring the wrong thing)
- Picks a small number of concrete failing traces as evidence — not just aggregate stats","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Rewriting a Prompt","scope_included":"- Produces a v2 prompt that addresses identified weaknesses while preserving the working parts
  - Adds missing sections (edge-case rules, output schema field definitions, examples) where the gap is real
  - Tightens vague instructions into concrete, testable rules
  - Adds 1-2 worked examples that cover the failing case_types
  - Does NOT delete or rewrite parts of the prompt that were working — minimal targeted changes
- Respects external constraints when rewriting
  - Output schema or downstream contract is non-negotiable unless the candidate flags a needed change explicitly
  - Style guide or brand voice constraints carry over
  - Length / latency budgets carry over
- Articulates the trade-off the rewrite makes
  - 'This v2 will be more verbose on edge cases but slightly slower on the happy path'
  - 'I added a refusal rule for unclear inputs — this will increase refusal rate on borderline cases'
- Knows that 'just be more careful' or 'be more accurate' is not a prompt change — every change must be a concrete instruction","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Comparing Prompt Variants (A/B)","scope_included":"- Reads an A/B test results file (per-case: variant A output, variant B output, judge scores, case_type) and forms a defensible recommendation
  - Uses the headline win rate as a starting signal, not the only signal
  - Computes per-case_type wins/losses to surface segment-level regressions
  - Spots when variant B wins on common cases but regresses on the high-risk minority
- Identifies weaknesses in the A/B test design itself
  - Sample is too narrow / skewed toward easy cases
  - Judge prompt scores only format match, not reasoning or safety
  - Metric used does not match the user-facing outcome
  - No segmentation — headline number could be hiding regressions
- Makes a concrete decision: ship A, ship B, ship neither (more iteration needed), or limited rollout
  - Ties the decision to specific evidence in the prompts and the per-case results
  - Names what they would watch for in production after rollout
- Proposes one specific next iteration — not a vague 'try more variants'","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Output Format and Guardrails","scope_included":"- Understands common output format choices and their failure modes
  - JSON schema with required fields — what happens when the model omits a required field, hallucinates a field, or returns invalid JSON
  - Free-text with length limits — what happens when the model exceeds the limit
  - Structured labels — what happens when the model returns a label not in the allowed set
- Knows when to add format constraints to the prompt vs handle in calling code
  - 'Validate JSON in code, but tell the model the schema in the prompt'
  - 'Don't ask the model to produce labels you have not listed'
- Understands basic guardrails as prompt instructions
  - Don't speculate when source data is missing
  - Don't reveal internal label names or scoring criteria
  - Refuse in named situations (legal, medical, abusive content) per product policy
- Recognises that overly strict guardrails can drive false refusals — there is a trade-off","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Communicating a Prompt Iteration","scope_included":"- Writes up a prompt iteration as a short, scannable note
  - What was wrong (1-3 specific issues, each tied to evidence)
  - What the v2 changes are (concrete bullet list — added X, tightened Y, added example Z)
  - What trade-off the v2 makes
  - What to watch in production after the change ships
- Avoids vague language ('made the prompt better', 'improved accuracy')
- Tags any change that requires alignment with another team (engineering for output schema changes, legal for guardrail changes, design for tone changes)
- Knows what a prompt iteration note is NOT — it is not a full eval framework, not an A/B test design, not a model-selection decision","is_non_core_section":false}]}
